If an attacker can compromise a translation file, malicious content can be injected into the application and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the user's browser.

This combination bypasses Angular's built-in sanitization, allowing attacker-controlled data bound to those attributes to inject and execute malicious JavaScript.

Handlebars is vulnerable to XSS via prototype pollution: the resolvePartial() function looks up partial names with an unguarded property access that traverses the prototype chain. If Object.prototype has been polluted with a string value matching a partial name, the polluted content is rendered unescaped, allowing cross-site scripting.

Handlebars is vulnerable to Remote Code Execution when Handlebars.compile() is passed an attacker-controlled pre-parsed AST object. The value field of a NumberLiteral AST node is emitted directly into the generated JavaScript without sanitization, allowing code injection that executes on the server.

Handlebars is vulnerable to Remote Code Execution via the @partial-block mechanism. When a helper with write access to the template data context overwrites @partial-block with a crafted Handlebars AST, subsequent invocation of that partial causes the runtime to dynamically compile the AST, executing arbitrary JavaScript on the server.

Handlebars has a time-of-check/time-of-use flaw in container.lookup() when the compat compile option is enabled. The function uses lookupProperty() as a security gate but then discards its result and performs a raw unguarded property access, bypassing prototype-access controls.

Handlebars is vulnerable to Denial of Service via unregistered decorator syntax. When a template contains a decorator reference to an unregistered decorator (e.g. {{*n}}), the compiled template attempts to invoke undefined as a function, causing an unhandled TypeError that crashes the Node.js process.

Handlebars is vulnerable to Remote Code Execution through dynamic partial lookups. A crafted object with call: true placed in the template context bypasses resolvePartial() guards and causes the runtime to compile the object as a Handlebars AST via env.compile(), executing injected JavaScript on the server.

The Handlebars CLI precompiler is vulnerable to code injection through multiple unsanitized inputs: template file names, the namespace (-n), CommonJS path (-c), and AMD path (-h) options are all interpolated into generated JavaScript without escaping. An attacker who controls these values can inject arbitrary JavaScript that executes when the generated bundle is loaded.

Handlebars has an asymmetric prototype method blocklist where __lookupSetter__ is omitted while its counterparts are blocked. When the non-default runtime option allowProtoMethodsByDefault is set to true, __lookupSetter__ becomes accessible in templates, creating an inconsistent security boundary.

DOMPurify 3.1.3 through 3.2.6 and 2.5.3 through 2.5.8 contain a cross-site scripting vulnerability that allows attackers to bypass attribute sanitization by exploiting missing textarea rawtext element validation in the SAFE_FOR_XML regex. Attackers can include closing rawtext tags like </textarea> in attribute values to break out of rawtext contexts and execute JavaScript when sanitized output is placed inside rawtext elements. The 3.x branch was fixed in 3.2.7; the 2.x branch was never patched.

When USE_PROFILES is enabled, DOMPurify rebuilds ALLOWED_ATTR as a plain array whose properties are looked up by name, making it susceptible to prototype pollution. If Array.prototype has been polluted with an event handler attribute name (e.g. onclick), DOMPurify will allow that event handler to survive sanitization, resulting in DOM-based XSS.

DOMPurify's ADD_ATTR predicate function mechanism (via EXTRA_ELEMENT_HANDLING.attributeCheck) short-circuits URI validation when the predicate returns true. This allows unsafe protocols such as javascript: to survive sanitization in href and similar attributes, enabling DOM-based XSS when such links are activated.

DOMPurify is vulnerable to mutation-XSS (mXSS) when sanitized HTML is embedded into special raw-text wrapper elements such as xmp, script, iframe, noembed, noframes, or noscript before being assigned via innerHTML. Attacker-controlled sequences like </xmp> inside attribute values close the raw-text context during the second parse, causing the sanitized output to mutate into executable markup.
DOMPurify 3.1.3-3.3.1 and 2.5.3-2.5.8 contain a cross-site scripting vulnerability allowing bypass of attribute sanitization via five missing rawtext elements in the SAFE_FOR_XML regex.

DOMPurify has a logic inconsistency where FORBID_TAGS is not checked when a function-based ADD_TAGS (tagCheck) returns true. Due to short-circuit evaluation, the FORBID_TAGS check is never evaluated, allowing explicitly forbidden elements to pass through sanitization when EXTRA_ELEMENT_HANDLING.tagCheck is configured.

DOMPurify has an asymmetric security inconsistency where FORBID_TAGS does not win over a function-based ADD_TAGS predicate, while FORBID_ATTR correctly wins over ADD_ATTR. When ADD_TAGS is provided as a function that returns true, explicitly forbidden tags such as iframe and form pass through sanitization with their attributes intact.

DOMPurify's SAFE_FOR_TEMPLATES feature fails to strip Mustache/template expressions when RETURN_DOM or RETURN_DOM_FRAGMENT is used. The final string-scrubbing pass that removes expressions like {{...}} is skipped in DOM-return mode, and split text nodes (e.g. from removed intermediate tags) can rejoin to form a complete expression that template-evaluating frameworks like Vue 2 will execute.

DOMPurify versions 3.0.1 through 3.3.3 are vulnerable to an XSS bypass via prototype pollution. When no CUSTOM_ELEMENT_HANDLING is specified, the fallback plain object inherits from Object.prototype; if an attacker has polluted Object.prototype.tagNameCheck and Object.prototype.attributeNameCheck with permissive regexes, DOMPurify allows arbitrary custom elements with event handler attributes through sanitization.

In Svelte's server-side rendering, a textarea element with a two-way bound value does not have its content properly escaped in the rendered HTML. Attacker-controlled values can break out of the textarea context and inject arbitrary HTML or script content into the page.
Svelte has a potential mXSS vulnerability due to improper HTML escaping

An XSS vulnerability exists in Svelte 5 when using the experimental hydratable function with attacker-controlled key values. The key is embedded into a server-rendered script block without escaping unsafe characters, allowing a malicious key to break out of the script context and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the client's browser.
In Svelte SSR, attribute spreading on elements enumerates prototype-chain properties rather than own properties, which can expose polluted Object.prototype attributes in rendered output.

Versions of svelte prior to 5.51.5 are vulnerable to cross-site scripting (XSS) during server-side rendering. When using spread syntax to render attributes from untrusted data, event handler properties are included in the rendered HTML output. If an application spreads user-controlled or external data as element attributes, an attacker can inject malicious event handlers that execute in victims' browsers.

When using `<svelte:element this={tag}>` in server-side rendering, the provided tag name is not validated or sanitized before being emitted into the HTML output. If the tag string contains unexpected characters, it can result in HTML injection in the SSR output. Client-side rendering is not affected.

In certain circumstances, the server-side rendering output of an `<option>` element does not properly escape its content, potentially allowing HTML injection in the SSR output. Client-side rendering is not affected.

The contents of `bind:innerText` and `bind:textContent` on `contenteditable` elements were not properly escaped. This could enable HTML injection and Cross-site Scripting (XSS) if rendering untrusted data as the binding's initial value on the server.

Errors from `transformError` were not correctly escaped prior to being embedded in the HTML output, causing potential HTML injection and XSS if attacker-controlled content is returned from `transformError`.

Svelte is vulnerable to XSS when spread syntax is used to render attributes from untrusted data, because event handler properties such as onclick are included in the server-rendered HTML output. An attacker who can control spread attributes can inject malicious event handlers that execute in victims' browsers before Svelte's hydration runs.

Svelte is vulnerable to DOM clobbering of its internal framework state when attribute spreading is used on form elements and the name attribute on child input or button elements is user-controlled. Simultaneous attacker-controlled spread and name attributes can corrupt Svelte's state and potentially lead to XSS.

Svelte's hydratable feature (experimental) does not properly stringify promise values when hydrating, potentially leading to XSS. Applications using hydratable with attacker-controlled input where a synchronous value is followed by a promise value are affected.
An internal regex in the Svelte runtime can take exponential time when processing svelte:element tags of unconstrained length, leading to ReDoS.

When Axios runs on Node.js and is given a data: scheme URL, it decodes the entire payload into memory and returns a synthetic 200 response, ignoring maxContentLength and maxBodyLength limits. An attacker can supply a very large data: URI to cause unbounded memory allocation and crash the process, constituting a Denial of Service.

The mergeConfig function in axios crashes with a TypeError when processing configuration objects containing __proto__ as an own property, such as those created via JSON.parse. An attacker who can pass a JSON payload with __proto__ as a config key can trigger a complete Denial of Service.

Axios does not correctly normalize hostnames when checking NO_PROXY rules, causing requests to loopback addresses with a trailing dot (localhost.) or IPv6 literals ([::1]) to bypass NO_PROXY matching and be routed through the configured proxy. Attackers controlling request URLs can exploit this to bypass SSRF mitigations and reach sensitive loopback or internal services.

Axios is vulnerable to being used as a gadget in a prototype pollution attack chain: when Object.prototype is polluted with CRLF-containing header values via another dependency, Axios merges the polluted properties into request headers without sanitization. This allows HTTP request smuggling attacks including bypassing AWS IMDSv2 session token requirements to steal cloud credentials.

Axios contains prototype pollution gadgets in request config processing; a polluted Object.prototype.transformResponse can be executed with access to request credentials and response data, enabling credential theft and response hijacking across all requests.

Axios does not enforce maxBodyLength for stream request bodies when maxRedirects is set to 0, because the native HTTP transport path used in that configuration does not perform byte counting. Oversized streamed uploads are sent in full despite the configured body size limit, enabling uncontrolled upstream data transfer and potential resource exhaustion.

The toFormData helper in Axios recursively walks nested objects without a depth limit or try/catch protection. An attacker who can provide a deeply nested object as request data or params can trigger a stack overflow, crashing the Node.js request handler or process.

Axios is vulnerable to a prototype pollution gadget that allows header injection in the Node.js HTTP adapter. When Object.prototype is polluted with duck-typed FormData properties including getHeaders, Axios treats any plain object payload as a FormData instance and calls the attacker-controlled getHeaders() function, merging arbitrary headers into the outgoing request.

The fix for the NO_PROXY hostname normalization bypass (GHSA-3p68-rc4w-qgx5) is incomplete: shouldBypassProxy() does not resolve loopback address equivalents. When NO_PROXY=localhost is set, requests to 127.0.0.1 and [::1] are still routed through the configured proxy, enabling SSRF bypasses against internal services.

Axios has multiple prototype pollution gadgets where config properties are read without hasOwnProperty guards. When Object.prototype is polluted by a co-dependency, an attacker can silently intercept and modify every JSON response (parseReviver gadget) or fully hijack the underlying HTTP transport to intercept credentials and headers (transport gadget).

The Axios 1.15.0 patch for NO_PROXY hostname normalization is incomplete: the isLoopback() function only recognizes three specific addresses (localhost, 127.0.0.1, ::1) but not the full RFC 1122 loopback subnet 127.0.0.0/8. Requests to any 127.x.x.x address other than 127.0.0.1 bypass NO_PROXY matching and are routed through the configured proxy, enabling SSRF.

Axios does not enforce maxContentLength when responseType is set to 'stream'. The response stream is returned directly without byte-counting, allowing unbounded downstream consumption of arbitrarily large server responses despite the configured limit.

Axios is vulnerable to a prototype pollution gadget via the validateStatus config property, which uniquely uses the JavaScript in operator (which traverses the prototype chain) during config merging. When Object.prototype.validateStatus is polluted with () => true, all HTTP error responses (401, 403, 500, etc.) are silently treated as successes, bypassing authentication and error handling.

The encode() function in Axios's AxiosURLSearchParams helper contains a character map entry that reverses the safe percent-encoding of null bytes: '%00' is mapped back to a raw null byte '\x00' after encodeURIComponent has correctly encoded it. Applications using AxiosURLSearchParams directly without an external encoder will produce URLs containing raw null bytes, which can cause truncation or WAF bypass in downstream C-based parsers.

Axios's XSRF token protection uses truthy/falsy semantics rather than strict boolean comparison for the withXSRFToken config property. When Object.prototype is polluted with any truthy non-boolean value for withXSRFToken, the same-origin check is bypassed and the XSRF token is sent to all cross-origin destinations, enabling CSRF attacks after token theft.

Axios 1.15.2 exposes two prototype pollution gadgets: HTTP header injection via utils.merge() building plain objects that walk the prototype chain, and a crash DoS where a polluted Object.prototype.get or .set causes TypeError on every axios request.

Next.js is vulnerable to HTTP request smuggling when proxying traffic through rewrites. A crafted DELETE or OPTIONS request using Transfer-Encoding: chunked can trigger request boundary disagreement between the Next.js proxy and the backend, allowing an attacker to smuggle requests to unintended backend routes including internal or admin endpoints.

The Next.js image optimization disk cache had no configurable upper bound, allowing unbounded growth. An attacker could generate many unique image optimization variants to exhaust disk space and cause Denial of Service, fixed by adding an LRU-backed disk cache with configurable maximum size.

A vulnerability in React Server Components (version 19.x) and Next.js 13.x-16.x App Router allows a specially crafted HTTP request to Server Function endpoints to trigger excessive CPU usage, resulting in Denial of Service.

Self-hosted Next.js applications are vulnerable to Denial of Service via the Image Optimization API for local images. The /_next/image endpoint fetches local images entirely into memory without enforcing a maximum response size, allowing an attacker to cause out-of-memory conditions by requesting large local assets matching the configured localPatterns.

Next.js Pages Router applications with i18n configured are vulnerable to middleware authorization bypass. Locale-less /_next/data/<buildId>/<page>.json requests do not trigger middleware execution, allowing attackers to retrieve server-side rendered JSON for protected pages without passing authorization checks.

Next.js is vulnerable to cache poisoning and Denial of Service via the x-nextjs-data request header. An external client sending this header on a request to a redirect-returning middleware path causes the standard Location header to be replaced with the internal x-nextjs-redirect header, which browsers do not follow; if a CDN caches the malformed response, all subsequent visitors to that path receive a broken redirect.

A vulnerability in React Server Components (version 19.x) and Next.js 13.x-16.x App Router allows a specially crafted HTTP request to Server Function endpoints to trigger excessive CPU usage, resulting in Denial of Service.

Next.js is vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting when beforeInteractive scripts are used with untrusted content. Serialized script content was not HTML-escaped before embedding into the document, allowing attacker-controlled input to break out of the inline script context and execute arbitrary JavaScript in visitors' browsers.

Next.js App Router applications using CSP nonces are vulnerable to stored Cross-Site Scripting when deployed behind shared caches. Malformed nonce values derived from request headers can be reflected into rendered HTML unsafely, allowing cache poisoning that causes script execution for subsequent visitors.

React Server Component responses in Next.js are vulnerable to cache poisoning when deployed with shared caches that have insufficient response partitioning. Collisions in the _rsc cache-busting value can allow an attacker to poison cache entries so users receive incorrect response variants for given URLs.

Self-hosted Next.js applications are vulnerable to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via crafted WebSocket upgrade requests. An attacker can cause the built-in Node.js server to proxy WebSocket connections to arbitrary internal or external destinations, potentially exposing internal services or cloud metadata endpoints.

Next.js App Router applications using React Server Components are vulnerable to cache poisoning when shared caches do not correctly partition response variants. An attacker can cause RSC payloads to be served from the original URL and poison shared cache entries so subsequent visitors receive component payloads instead of the expected HTML.

Next.js applications using Partial Prerendering via the Cache Components feature are vulnerable to connection exhaustion Denial of Service. Crafted POST requests to a server action with the Next-Resume header can trigger a request-body handling deadlock that keeps connections open indefinitely, consuming file descriptors until legitimate users are denied service.

Next.js App Router applications using middleware for authorization are vulnerable to bypass via transport-specific route variants used for segment prefetching. Specially crafted .rsc and segment-prefetch URLs resolve to the same page without triggering middleware matching rules, allowing protected content to be accessed without authorization checks.

Next.js App Router applications relying on middleware to protect dynamic routes are vulnerable to authorization bypass via crafted query parameters. Specially crafted parameters can alter the dynamic route value seen by the page rendering logic while leaving the visible URL path unchanged, causing middleware to match a different route than the page actually renders.

The fix for CVE-2026-44575 (middleware authorization bypass) was not applied to middleware.ts when using Turbopack. Applications using Turbopack for compilation remain vulnerable to the same middleware bypass as described in that advisory.

A vulnerability in React Server Components (versions 19.0.0-19.2.1) and Next.js 15.x/16.x App Router allows a specially crafted HTTP request to cause the server process to hang and consume CPU during deserialization, resulting in Denial of Service.

A vulnerability in React Server Components (versions 19.0.0-19.2.1) and Next.js 15.x/16.x App Router allows a malicious HTTP request to retrieve the compiled source code of Server Functions, potentially exposing business logic.

The fix for CVE-2025-55184 in React Server Components was incomplete. An incomplete patch in React 19.0.2, 19.1.3, and 19.2.2 and Next.js 13.x-16.x App Router allows a specially crafted HTTP request to Server Function endpoints to trigger an infinite loop, causing CPU exhaustion and Denial of Service.

A Denial of Service vulnerability in Next.js affects applications with Partial Prerendering (PPR) enabled in minimal mode. The unauthenticated PPR resume endpoint accepts arbitrary POST requests without enforcing size limits on the request body or decompressed payload size, allowing an attacker to exhaust server memory via large payloads or zipbomb attacks and crash the Node.js process.

A vulnerability in React Server Components packages (versions 19.0.x-19.2.x) and Next.js 13.x-16.x App Router allows a specially crafted HTTP request to Server Function endpoints to trigger excessive CPU usage, out-of-memory exceptions, or server crashes, resulting in Denial of Service.

A vulnerability in React Server Components packages (versions 19.0.0, 19.1.0, 19.1.1, 19.2.0) and Next.js 15.x/16.x App Router allows a malicious HTTP request to cause server-side issues. The affected packages are react-server-dom-parcel, react-server-dom-turbopack, and react-server-dom-webpack.

The /_next/image endpoint loads external images entirely into memory without a size limit, allowing an attacker to exhaust server memory by requesting optimization of arbitrarily large images from allowed domains.

A Denial of Service vulnerability in Next.js App Router with Partial Prerendering enabled allows attackers to send oversized next-resume POST payloads in non-minimal deployments. The previous mitigation only covered minimal-mode deployments, leaving equivalent non-minimal deployments vulnerable to unbounded request body buffering and excessive memory consumption.

In Next.js development mode (next dev), cross-site protection for internal WebSocket endpoints treated Origin: null as a bypass case even when allowedDevOrigins was configured. This allows opaque/sandboxed contexts to connect to the HMR WebSocket channel if the dev server is reachable from attacker-controlled content.

Next.js Server Action CSRF validation treated the string origin value 'null' as a missing origin rather than a cross-origin request. Attackers could exploit this by inducing victims to submit Server Actions from sandboxed iframe contexts, bypassing origin verification and potentially executing state-changing actions with victim credentials.

Lodash versions 4.0.0 through 4.17.22 are vulnerable to prototype pollution via the _.unset and _.omit functions. An attacker can pass crafted paths to delete methods from global prototypes such as Object.prototype, though the issue only permits deletion and not overwriting of properties.

Lodash 4.17.23 and earlier are vulnerable to a prototype pollution bypass in _.unset and _.omit. The fix for CVE-2025-13465 only guards against string key members, so attackers can bypass it by passing array-wrapped path segments to delete properties from built-in prototypes including Object.prototype, Number.prototype, and String.prototype.

Lodash _.template is vulnerable to code injection via unsanitized options.imports key names. Untrusted key names are passed to the Function() constructor sink without validation, and the use of assignInWith (which enumerates inherited properties) also means that pre-existing prototype pollution on Object.prototype can flow into the Function() sink and execute arbitrary code at template compilation time.

Versions of three.js before 0.125.0 are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) when handling rgb or hsl color strings. Specially crafted color strings with large amounts of whitespace cause catastrophic backtracking in the color parsing regex, allowing an attacker to block the event loop.

Affected versions of highlight.js are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution. A malicious HTML code block with a crafted language name can pollute the base object's prototype during syntax highlighting, potentially causing application instability or crashes (Denial of Service) in applications that render user-provided code blocks.

Affected versions of highlight.js are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution. A malicious HTML code block with a crafted language name can pollute the base object's prototype during syntax highlighting, potentially causing application instability or crashes (Denial of Service) in applications that render user-provided code blocks.

Multiple grammars in highlight.js prior to version 10.4.1 contain regular expressions with exponential or polynomial backtracking that can be exploited for Denial of Service. Affected grammars include C, Perl, JavaScript, TypeScript, Ruby, C#, Markdown, and others; applications that highlight user-provided content with any of these grammars are vulnerable to event loop blocking or server hangs.

Versions of marked 0.3.0 and earlier are vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via two vectors: GFM codeblocks and JavaScript URLs. These attacks bypass the sanitize: true setting and allow injection of arbitrary HTML and script content.

Versions of marked 0.3.2 and earlier are vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via vbscript: URLs in IE10 compatibility mode. The sanitize: true setting does not prevent vbscript: protocol links from being rendered, enabling script execution when victims click the crafted link.

Versions of marked 0.3.3 and earlier are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) via the em (emphasis) inline rule. Input reaching this rule can trigger catastrophic backtracking and block the event loop.

Affected versions of marked are vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in link components when sanitize: true is configured. HTML entities in link URIs are processed and decoded in a way that allows protocols like javascript: to survive sanitization, executing scripts when the link is clicked.
marked version 0.3.6 and earlier is vulnerable to an XSS attack in the data: URI parser.

Affected versions of marked are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) with significant amplification: 1,000 characters of crafted input can block the event loop for approximately 6 seconds.

Marked prior to version 0.3.17 is vulnerable to a Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) attack due to catastrophic backtracking in several regular expressions used for parsing HTML tags and markdown links. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by providing specially crafted markdown input, such as deeply nested or repetitively structured brackets or tag attributes, which cause the parser to hang and lead to a Denial of Service.

Marked versions 0.3.14 through 0.6.1 are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) in email address parsing. Email addresses embedded in Markdown may be evaluated in quadratic time, potentially crashing the Node.js process through resource exhaustion.

Affected versions of marked are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) via the _label subrule. Malformed input can significantly degrade parsing performance, blocking the event loop.

Marked versions prior to 2.0.0 are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS). Processing user-generated Markdown input can trigger catastrophic backtracking in the parser's regular expressions, causing the event loop to block or the server to hang.

Marked is vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) via the inline.reflinkSearch regex rule. A crafted Markdown string containing deeply nested bracket patterns causes catastrophic backtracking, blocking the event loop and potentially causing Denial of Service for applications processing untrusted Markdown.

Marked is vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) via the block.def regex rule. A crafted Markdown string with repeated spaces in link definition syntax causes catastrophic backtracking, blocking the event loop and causing Denial of Service for applications processing untrusted Markdown.

Marked 18.0.0 is vulnerable to Denial of Service via memory exhaustion from infinite recursion. A specific 3-byte input (tab, vertical tab, newline) causes an inescapable cycle between the block tokenizer and inline tokenizer that allocates unbounded memory until the Node.js heap limit is reached, crashing the process.

The merge and clone helper methods in zrender's src/core/util.ts module are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution. Malicious input passed to these methods can add or modify properties on Object.prototype, affecting zrender and downstream consumers such as Apache ECharts which exports these methods directly.

The merge and clone helper methods in zrender's src/core/util.ts module are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution. Malicious input passed to these methods can add or modify properties on Object.prototype, affecting zrender and downstream consumers such as Apache ECharts which exports these methods directly.

Versions of video.js before 7.14.3 are vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS). The src attribute of track tags does not properly escape HTML, allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution via crafted track source values.

identifiers.summary: Versions of Quill prior to 1.3.7 are vulnerable to Reverse Tabnabbing. The package uses target='_blank' in anchor tags without rel='noopener', allowing pages opened via Quill links to access and manipulate the window.opener of the original page, commonly exploited in phishing attacks.

A stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability in Quill's HTML editor allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript by inserting a crafted onloadstart attribute on an IMG element. The behavior is disputed as potentially intentional browser behavior rather than a product vulnerability.
Quill 2.0.3 is vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via insufficient data validation in the HTML export feature, allowing injected HTML content to execute in the browser.
Versions of the package swiper before 6.5.1 are susceptible to prototype pollution.

Swiper versions >=6.5.1 and <12.1.2 are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution that bypasses a previous fix. The indexOf() check used to block forbidden keys can be neutralized by polluting Array.prototype.indexOf, allowing crafted input passed to extendDefaults() to add or modify properties on Object.prototype.

MooTools is a collection of JavaScript utilities for JavaScript developers. All known versions include a CSS selector parser that is vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS). An attack requires that an attacker can inject a string into a CSS selector at runtime, which is quite common with e.g. jQuery CSS selectors. No patches are available for this issue.
This affects all versions of package mootools. This is due to the ability to pass untrusted input to Object.merge()
Versions of protobufjs before 5.0.3 and 6.8.6 are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) when parsing crafted invalid .proto files.
Versions of protobufjs before 5.0.3 and 6.8.6 are vulnerable to Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) when parsing crafted invalid .proto files.

protobufjs versions 6.10.0-6.10.2 and 6.11.0-6.11.2 are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution. An attacker can pollute Object.prototype by providing untrusted input to util.setProperty or ReflectionObject.setParsedOption, or by causing the application to parse or load malicious .proto files.

protobufjs versions 6.10.0-6.10.2 and 6.11.0-6.11.2 are vulnerable to Prototype Pollution. An attacker can pollute Object.prototype by providing untrusted input to util.setProperty or ReflectionObject.setParsedOption, or by causing the application to parse or load malicious .proto files.

protobufjs 6.10.0-6.11.3 and 7.0.0-7.2.3 allow Prototype Pollution via user-controlled protobuf messages, enabling attackers to pollute Object.prototype through parse, load/loadSync, or setParsedOption/setProperty functions.

protobufjs 6.10.0-6.11.3 and 7.0.0-7.2.3 allow Prototype Pollution via user-controlled protobuf messages, enabling attackers to pollute Object.prototype through parse, load/loadSync, or setParsedOption/setProperty functions.

protobufjs is vulnerable to Remote Code Execution when loading crafted JSON descriptors. Schema-controlled type names and type references reach runtime code generation without sufficient validation, allowing an attacker who can influence the loaded schema to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the process.

protobufjs is vulnerable to Denial of Service via crafted protobuf schemas or JSON descriptors. Control characters in field names are not escaped before being embedded into generated JavaScript function bodies, causing runtime code generation to throw a syntax error and making affected message types unusable.

protobufjs is vulnerable to Remote Code Execution via crafted protobuf descriptors. A non-string default value for a bytes field in an attacker-controlled descriptor can cause unsafe code to be emitted into the generated toObject conversion function, executing arbitrary JavaScript when the function is called.

protobufjs is vulnerable to Denial of Service via stack overflow when decoding deeply nested protobuf data. The decoder recurses without a depth limit when processing nested group fields or message fields, allowing a crafted protobuf binary payload to exhaust the JavaScript call stack.

protobufjs is vulnerable to a prototype pollution gadget where internal type lookup tables use plain objects that inherit from Object.prototype. If Object.prototype has been polluted by another vulnerability, attacker-controlled strings can be incorporated into generated encode or decode JavaScript functions, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution.

protobufjs generated message constructors copy enumerable properties from a provided properties object without filtering the __proto__ key. An attacker who can control the properties object passed to a generated message constructor can modify the prototype chain of the resulting message instance, potentially affecting downstream instanceof checks or inherited property lookups.

protobufjs is vulnerable to Denial of Service via prototype pollution in schema option processing. Crafted protobuf schemas with option paths that traverse inherited object properties can write to global JavaScript constructor properties, corrupting process-wide built-in functionality and causing persistent failures for the lifetime of the affected process.

protobufjs's minimal UTF-8 decoder accepts overlong byte sequences and decodes them to their canonical characters rather than replacing them with the Unicode replacement character. This can allow attackers to bypass application-level byte-pattern filters, since bytes that do not match forbidden ASCII characters may decode to strings containing those characters.

protobufjs allows unbounded recursion when expanding nested JSON descriptors via Root.fromJSON() and Namespace.addJSON(), enabling stack overflow denial-of-service via crafted deeply-nested JSON descriptors.
